title: Internal versus external wall insulation for solid walls
subtitle: Comparing IWI and EWI to choose the right approach
excerpt: Solid-wall houses (typically pre-1930s UK construction) can be insulated from the inside or outside. Each approach has different planning implications, thermal performance, and disruption. The choice is often dictated by the building rather than preference.
sourceNotes: Historic England guidance on internal and external insulation for traditional buildings. Energy Saving Trust solid-wall insulation guidance. CIBSE TM54 on thermal bridge treatment in retrofits. gov.uk ECO4 and GBIS eligibility guidance.

content.text: A solid-wall house has a wall
U-value

of approximately 2.1 W/m²·K for 220 mm brick: about twice the heat loss through a cavity-wall house of the same period. The two retrofit routes are internal wall insulation (IWI) and external wall insulation (EWI). Both reach broadly the same target U-value; the differences lie in planning, disruption, thermal performance, and moisture behaviour.
Internal wall insulation

IWI is applied room by room to the inner face of external walls. It does not change the facade. The insulation (typically 60 mm PIR board or 100 mm mineral wool stud-frame system) reduces room size by 60 to 100 mm per treated wall. In a typical Victorian terrace, treating all external walls reduces the floor area of each room by 3 to 6 percent.
A
vapour control layer

is required on the warm face. The original wall is now cold: if it was previously lime-plastered and breathable, trapping it behind vapour-impermeable insulation changes its moisture dynamics. A hygroscopic VCL or a breathable system with mineral wool is the correct approach for lime-built walls.
IWI leaves
thermal bridges
at every floor and ceiling junction where the insulation is interrupted by the structure. It also requires relocating sockets, radiators, and pipework on each treated wall.
External wall insulation
EWI fixes insulation boards to the outer face of the wall and applies render or cladding over the top. It does not reduce room size. It wraps the building continuously, eliminating the repeating
solid wall insulation
challenge of thermal bridges at floor junctions. The wall itself moves to the warm side of the insulation, improving thermal mass effectiveness and protecting the fabric from freeze-thaw damage.

EWI thickens the building by 80 to 250 mm on each treated face. On street-facing or shared walls, this raises planning questions. In a conservation area, front-elevation EWI typically requires planning permission. On a listed building, EWI is not permitted.
When to choose IWI
Listed building: EWI is not permitted. IWI is the only option and requires listed-building consent.
Front of the house in a conservation area. EWI will likely need planning permission on the front. IWI avoids this.
Shared party walls. EWI cannot run onto the neighbour's property. A mid-terrace end gable is one example.
Room-by-room phased work: IWI can be done one room at a time alongside decoration. EWI is almost always a whole-house project.
When to choose EWI
Rear and side walls outside a conservation area. These are usually permitted development. EWI on the back of a terrace avoids the planning issue on the front.
Where you need to keep the room size as it is. A narrow Victorian terrace with small rooms loses real floor area to IWI on both walls of a side room.
Where the outside render is breaking down anyway. EWI replaces the render and insulates in one go.

attrs.body: EWI on the rear and IWI on the front is the most common practical solution for terraced houses in conservation areas. The EWI handles the main heat-loss wall (often the rear with larger rooms) and eliminates floor-junction bridges on that face; the IWI handles the front wall where planning constraints prevent EWI. A PAS 2035 whole-house survey is the correct starting point before commissioning either approach.
attrs.title: Combining both approaches
